metrics.narrative: Business problem is well-articulated but not quantified. Jonathan described operational pain across three areas on 15 May: (1) Contract/renewal bottlenecks caused by organizational segmentation ('separating out the contracting activities and category management... really bottlenecking things and taking time... the time to action a renewal or a contract are pretty ridiculous'), (2) Poor data quality from manual entry ('they're putting in junk... just putting in data to put in data... the reports end up being not that great'), and (3) Inefficient staff utilization ('how do you augment and make your staff more efficient through those use of tools like AI... using a higher level license for their work time'). He quantified clinical-side AI ROI as a parallel (Dragon Copilot for EHR documentation reduces physician typing burden) but did not quantify procurement pain (no cycle-time baseline, no cost-per-sourcing-event, no savings target, no headcount relief metric). On 26 Jun demo Jonathan confirmed interest in 'operational benefit: reduce manual sourcing workload for high-volume low-value items and speed buyer decision-making via conversational intake' but again did not specify volume, cost, or time saved. Gap: Zycus offered an ROI model on 6 May ('we use your data points... we have a data sheet, you populate it') but Jonathan has not provided the spend/volume data 51 days later, blocking quantification. Next move: secure the data input and generate a conservative ROI model anchored to Intermountain's actual tail-spend volume, contract renewal count, and category-team capacity to make the case quantifiable and executive-ready.

champion.narrative: Jonathan Kempton is a developing, single-threaded champion with access to decision-makers but no delivery on introductions in 51 days, signaling weak execution or deprioritization. Evidence of advocacy: Jonathan verbally committed on 6 May to facilitate VP and business-apps leadership introductions ('I'll introduce you to the VP over our business apps and some of the directors there... Mike Harmar and Robin Funcannon'), confirmed interest in the solution across three calls (6 May, 15 May, 26 Jun), attended a demo on 26 Jun after rescheduling (no-show 28 May, rescheduled 29 May for 22 Jun then moved to 26 Jun), and agreed to 'socialize demo and materials with the relevant stakeholder team and other stakeholder groups' (26 Jun). He actively shaped the demo agenda by articulating use cases (autonomous sourcing for tail spend, intake via Teams, integration with Workday/Conga). However, execution is weak: 51 days since the 6 May intro-VP commitment with zero follow-through, no VP meeting scheduled, no business-apps leadership engaged, and no spend data provided for ROI modeling despite Zycus request on 6 May. Jonathan no-showed the 28 May demo (cited 'out of the office and tied up with an internal project' per 22 Jun Next Step), creating a 29-day gap until reconnection, signaling competing priorities. His 26 Jun statement 'no immediate architecture changes expected through 2026' and his advisory-only role ('not directly influencing... recommendations and advice') mean he cannot drive a decision—he can only evangelize. Current engagement: active as of 26 Jun demo (2 days ago) but passive on next steps (no VP intro scheduled, no data delivered, no follow-up meeting set). Risk: single-threaded, weak champion who is interested but not accountable, with no Economic Buyer or Decision Maker access secured in nearly 2 months. Developing rating is optimistic; execution to date is closer to weak. What is missing: Jonathan must deliver the VP intro and the ROI-model data input within 7 days (by 5 Jul) or he is a blocker, not a champion.

competition.narrative: Competition is the status quo / inertia anchored in Workday ERP recency and organizational flux, not a named peer vendor. Jonathan explicitly stated on 15 May that Intermountain 'just rolled out Workday to replace two separate ERPs... only about a month and a half after our go live... still a little rocky' and 'probably a year, two years into maybe talking about making very huge changes to that portfolio' due to Workday stabilization needs and supply-chain reorganization (CPO turnover). Workday is not a direct competitor to Zycus (it is the ERP that Zycus would integrate with) but the recent go-live creates a strong bias toward letting Workday mature before layering on additional procurement tools, making the status quo the true competitive threat. Jonathan confirmed on 26 Jun 'no immediate architecture changes expected through 2026,' meaning any Zycus deployment would compete with the organizational decision to defer platform changes until post-Workday stabilization (2027 at earliest). Conga CLM is the incumbent contract-management tool ('Conga CLM solution... bolt on for contracts') but Jonathan expressed dissatisfaction ('high level of required fields... they're putting in junk... reports end up being not that great') and noted it was selected by a departed stakeholder ('the person who was spearheading that process is no longer with Intermountain'), suggesting Conga is under review but not actively defended. Vizient, the GPO, was mentioned on 15 May as providing 'procedural analytics' and spend-analysis services ('they come in and layer on services on top of some of these products'), positioning Vizient as a potential competitor in the spend-analytics module but not in intake or autonomous sourcing. No other named competitors (Coupa, SAP Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) mentioned in any call or field. The competitive wedge is urgency: prove that acting now (pilot ANA for tail-spend savings, pilot intake for user-experience improvement) delivers ROI that justifies moving before Workday stabilization is complete, rather than waiting 12-18 months and risking another vendor filling the gap or the pain becoming normalized.

identify_pain.narrative: Three specific operational pains confirmed by Jonathan Kempton with ownership clear. Pain 1: Contract/renewal bottleneck from organizational dysfunction. Jonathan stated on 15 May: 'Organizationally, the pain point was separating out the contracting activities and the category management activities. They created a sourcing center of excellence that was supposed to do the contracting piece, and it ended up really bottlenecking things and taking time... from a business perspective, kind of as a consumer of the supply chain service, the time to action a renewal or a contract are pretty ridiculous. They have to set very low expectations for people.' Owner: supply chain leadership (sourcing center of excellence and category management, post-CPO-turnover reorganization in flux). Pain 2: Poor data quality from manual Conga CLM entry reducing reporting value. Jonathan on 15 May: 'The Conga CLM tool... there's a high level of required fields... as they're entering data just to get to the next step... they're putting in junk... just putting in data to put in data... the reports end up being not that great. They're not worth what it was to take it.' Owner: category leaders and sourcing specialists using Conga CLM. Pain 3: Inefficient staff utilization (manual work consuming high-value time). Jonathan on 15 May: 'The biggest thing would be how do you augment and make your staff more efficient through those use of tools like AI... you could say where possible you utilize AI as a solution to grab the things that you need out of contracts or out of negotiations and make it so that it's kind of already presented to the category leader or sourcing specialist on a platter to say hey you can just verify these things... it's a lot different than data entry and using a higher level license for their work time.' Owner: category-management and sourcing teams. All three pains tie to Jonathan's broader observation that supply chain is 'undergoing a lot of change and some difficulties' post-CPO departure and that Workday immaturity adds friction ('pretty new and working through the kinks'). These are confirmed, buyer-articulated pains, not inferred.

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is partially mapped from Jonathan's statements but not validated by the actual decision-makers. The process Jonathan described across calls: (1) Jonathan evangelizes internally ('share this stuff, have an internal meeting with that VP and the supply chain groups that I interact with' per 15 May; 'socialize demo and materials with the relevant stakeholder team and other stakeholder groups' per 26 Jun); (2) Business Applications team (Mike Harmar, Robin Funcannon) evaluates technologies and owns strategy; (3) Procurement/supply chain stakeholders and governance/leadership are involved during fiscal planning (Aug-Sep window for following calendar year, per 26 Jun); (4) VP of DTS Business Operations likely approves budget (Jonathan's boss, not yet engaged); (5) No immediate architecture changes expected through 2026 ('no immediate deployment planned; prospect unlikely to change architecture during 2026' per 26 Jun notes), so formal evaluation/approval would occur in Aug-Sep 2026 planning cycle for 2027 deployment. The call sequence itself shows a slow education/exploration motion: intro call 6 May, technical discovery 15 May, demo attempt 28 May (no-show), rescheduled 29 May, demo executed 26 Jun—54 days to complete a demo, signaling low urgency and no forcing function. Jonathan has not executed on his 6 May verbal commitment to facilitate VP introductions in 51 days, indicating either deprioritization or internal political friction. Gap: we have not spoken to anyone with approval authority, so the actual approval chain (VP → business-apps VP → CFO? new CPO? governance committee?) is unconfirmed. Does the buyer need multi-level approval? Unknown. Next move: map the approval chain explicitly when the VP meeting occurs—ask who signs, who has veto power, and what the approval sequence looks like for a $200k procurement-platform spend.

decision_criteria.narrative: Evaluation criteria are partially articulated from Jonathan's discovery responses and demo interaction, but not formalized or validated by decision-making stakeholders. From the 15 May and 26 Jun calls the buyer's stated evaluation lens includes: (1) Integration flexibility with Workday (ERP, must hand off PR/PO/AP seamlessly) and Conga CLM (contract metadata for utilization reporting), confirmed as non-negotiable ('obviously you have to... work through... the technical advice and recommendation'); (2) AI/automation capability for high-volume low-value sourcing ('autonomous sourcing... interesting... if we can show success it's a non-FTE way to address volume') and tail-spend benchmarking ('reseller RFQ scenarios'); (3) User experience improvement over current Workday intake ('negative feedback... the experience is heavy, clunky') via conversational/Teams-native interface; (4) Change-management minimization ('integration flexibility to handle partial overlays... minimize change management'); (5) Proof points/references from healthcare peers ('white papers, success stories... I can easily pass over to both our IT group and supply chain group to start to build interest'). Jordan (Zycus) positioned fit on 26 Jun: 'flexible integration... we can be a partial overlay... leave payments in Workday... metadata from Conga into Zycus for utilization tracking,' and Jonathan responded positively ('that makes sense'). However, these are Jonathan's advisory-level criteria, not the Business Applications VP's or the new CPO's decision framework. Gap: the actual decision-makers (VP DTS Biz Ops, Mike Harmar, Robin Funcannon, new CPO) have not articulated their criteria, priorities, or non-negotiables. Next move: when the VP meeting happens, ask them to walk through their platform-selection criteria, weighting, and must-haves vs nice-to-haves, and map Zycus's differentiation to their exact scorecard.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Hungry. Jonathan explicitly stated on 6 May 'we've jumped in the deep end... to figure out which direction to swim' when asked about organizational AI appetite, signaling high executive commitment to AI adoption. He cited clinical-side AI success (Dragon Copilot for EHR documentation reducing physician typing burden) as proof of organizational AI maturity and paralleled it to procurement AI opportunity on 15 May. He prioritized AI-driven autonomous sourcing ('if we can show success it's a non-FTE way to address volume') and staff-augmentation use cases ('how do you augment and make your staff more efficient through AI') over manual-process improvements, confirming AI is a strategic lever, not a nice-to-have. This aligns with Zycus's AI-first positioning (Merlin intake, ANA autonomous negotiation agent, AI-driven spend classification) and creates a strong wedge vs status-quo / manual competitors. However, the AI story is under-activated (missing proof points and ROI model per prior sections).

best_practice_check.summary: Three critical win-strategy levers to activate now: (1) Break single-threading and engage VP/business-apps leadership this week to establish executive sponsorship and discover the Economic Buyer, (2) Deliver overdue proof points (white papers, success stories, ROI model) immediately so Jonathan can evangelize internally with credible collateral, (3) Create urgency via a no-cost 4-week ANA pilot that delivers measurable tail-spend savings before Aug-Sep fiscal planning window, converting passive interest into a funded 2027 roadmap item. competitive_position.summary: Status quo / inertia is the strongest current threat, anchored in Workday ERP recency (go-live Apr 2026, 1.5 months post-live as of 6 May call) and organizational flux (CPO turnover, supply chain reorganization). Jonathan explicitly stated on 15 May 'probably a year, two years into maybe talking about making very huge changes to that portfolio' and on 26 Jun confirmed 'no immediate architecture changes expected through 2026.' This makes deferral the default decision unless Zycus creates urgency via a compelling near-term ROI (pilot ANA for tail-spend savings, pilot intake for UX improvement) that justifies acting before Workday stabilization. Conga CLM (incumbent contract tool) is under implicit review due to Jonathan's dissatisfaction ('putting in junk data... reports not that great') but not actively threatened by a named replacement; Conga's threat level is low/dormant (not competing for same budget as Zycus, and Jonathan's pain creates an opening for Zycus CLM overlay or metadata integration). Vizient (GPO) provides spend-analysis services and is a potential competitor in the iAnalyze module but not in intake or autonomous sourcing; Vizient threat level is low/narrow (only spend analytics, and Jonathan's interest in Zycus autonomous sourcing suggests Vizient does not cover that use case). No other named competitors. Ranked by current threat: (1) Status quo / defer to 2027 = high threat (buyer's stated default), (2) Vizient spend-analytics services = low threat (narrow module overlap), (3) Conga CLM = dormant (incumbent under review, not competitive for Zycus's intake/sourcing use cases).

value.how_we_win: Build a quantified, board-level ROI case (savings, FTE capacity gain, user-satisfaction lift) that justifies acting now rather than waiting 12-18 months. Position Zycus as a low-change-management overlay (Teams intake, flexible integration, modular adoption) that de-risks the investment and accelerates value realization during Workday stabilization period, not after it.

value.name: Vizient (GPO)
value.quote: Jonathan Kempton on 15 May 2026: 'Vizient is our GPO... they come in and both layer on services on top of some of these products... procedural analytics... firms to come in and take a look and find spend.'
value.source: Avoma 15 May technical discovery call, Jonathan describing Vizient's spend-analysis services
value.how_we_win: Differentiate Zycus iAnalyze on automation, real-time dashboards, and Workday integration (Vizient is SOW-based consulting, not a self-service platform). Position autonomous sourcing (ANA) as a capability Vizient does not provide (Vizient finds spend, Zycus automates the sourcing event and negotiation to capture it).

value.name: Vizient (GPO)
value.quote: Vizient is our GPO, and they come in and layer on services on top of some of these products. Have you ever heard of procedural analytics from Vizient? That does some of spend analytics.
value.source: Avoma 15 May 2026, Jonathan
value.how_we_win: Vizient provides healthcare-specific benchmarking and some spend analytics as a service overlay, but is not a competitive S2P platform for intake, autonomous sourcing, or contract/workflow management. Position Zycus iAnalyze as broader and more flexible (covers indirect spend like software/professional services, integrates with Workday/Conga, provides self-service dashboards) and offer to complement or replace Vizient analytics with a platform Intermountain controls. Low current threat.
